---
output: html_fragment
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Understanding the SEIQHR Model

<img src="model_flow.png" alt="SEIQHR Model Flow Diagram" style="width:100%; max-width:600px; display:block; margin:auto;"/>

This simulation tracks the progression of an infection through a
population using the following differential equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= -\lambda S \\
\frac{dE}{dt} &= \lambda S - (\alpha + \omega) E \\
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \omega E - (p + \gamma_I) I \\
\frac{dH}{dt} &= p I - \gamma_H H \\
\frac{dQ}{dt} &= \alpha E + \gamma_H H + \gamma_I I - \gamma_Q Q \\
\frac{dR}{dt} &= \gamma_Q Q + \gamma_H H + \gamma_I I
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the force of infection ($\lambda$) is defined as:

$$
\lambda = \frac{\beta I + \epsilon \beta Q}{N}
$$

### Model Parameters

The table below defines the parameters used in the SEIQR model to
simulate transmission dynamics, isolation efficacy, and recovery rates.

| Parameter | Description |
|:-----------------------------------|:-----------------------------------|
| $\beta$ | Community transmission rate |
| $\epsilon$ | Transmission leakage factor from quarantined individuals |
| $\alpha$ | Rate at which exposed individuals are moved into quarantine |
| $\omega$ | Rate at which exposed individuals move to the infectious community |
| $p$ | Rate of detection and hospitalization for infectious individuals |
| $\delta$ | Rate of leakage from quarantine back to the susceptible population |
| $\gamma_I$ | Recovery rate for the infectious population |
| $\gamma_H$ | Recovery rate for the hospitalized population |
| $\gamma_Q$ | Recovery rate for the quarantined population |

## Model Explanation

The core of the model is the Force of Infection ($\lambda$), which
represents the probability of a susceptible person becoming infected.
Notably, this model calculates risk from two sources: the general
infectious population ($I$) and "quarantine leakage" from the
quarantined population ($I_q$). This allows you to simulate how
effective quarantine measures are at stopping spread versus how often
quarantined individuals still manage to interact with others.

The population flows follow these distinct paths:Exposure: Susceptible
($S$) individuals transition to the Exposed ($E_n$) state based on
$\lambda$.

**Decision Node**: Once exposed, the population splits. A portion is
successfully diverted into Quarantine ($I_q$) based on the quarantine
rate ($q$), while the remainder becomes Infectious ($I$) in the
community.Clinical Progression: The Infectious group ($I$) faces two
outcomes: being detected and moved to Hospitalization ($H$) at rate $p$
where the risk of community transmission is eliminated while the
recieved care or recovering naturally at rate $\gamma_I$. Recovery: The
model features three distinct recovery paths. All infected
compartments—Infectious ($I$), Hospitalized ($H$), and Quarantined
($I_q$)—eventually flow into the Removed ($R$) category. This represents
the acquisition of immunity, meaning these individuals are no longer
part of the transmission cycle.

**Note** it is importantant to note that during contact tracing, you
will quarantine people who you suspect may have been exposed. Therefore
the number quarantined here does not represent the number you will
quarantine on the field. You must adjust for your ability to detect
individuals who had been exposed( i.e have had effective contact with
case)

### Compartmental Transitions

1.  **The Exposure Split:** Upon becoming exposed ($E_n$), the
    population is divided. The rate $q$ determines the success of your
    quarantine policy in diverting exposed individuals away from the
    general infectious pool ($I$).
2.  **Clinical Burden:** The infectious population ($I$) represents the
    active burden on community health. The parameter $p$ allows for the
    simulation of testing and detection efforts that move individuals
    from the community into the Hospitalized ($H$) category.
3.  **Recovery and Immunity:** A critical feature of this model is that
    all clinical and isolation pathways—Infectious, Hospitalized, and
    Quarantined—eventually consolidate into the Removed ($R$) category.
    This represents a state of recovery where individuals have acquired
    immunity and can no longer contract or transmit the disease.

### Policy Implications

This model is highly sensitive to the interaction between quarantine
rate ($q$) and leakage ($\epsilon$). By adjusting these, you can observe
how a "leaky" quarantine might still be beneficial if the detection rate
($p$) is high, or conversely, how a high quarantine rate can
significantly flatten the infection curve by keeping individuals out of
the infectious ($I$) compartment. This model assumes that individuals in
the quarantined compartment ($Q$) can re-enter the susceptible pool
($S$) at a rate of $\delta$. This reflects scenarios where quarantine is
not permanent or where individuals exit the system while still
susceptible to future infection. Conversely, individuals who
successfully complete the quarantine process recover and move to the $R$
compartment.
